_meta.note: Regression reference for BikeMedic. Re-run each case's input against the live backend (npm run check:golden bike-medic) and diff for QUALITY not exact match: all sections present, non-empty arrays stay non-empty, no truncation. The DIAGNOSIS case GUARDS the currency-localization fix: input is en-GB/GBP and parts_cost MUST be in GBP (£), NOT USD ($) — Types 1&2 now append withLocaleContext and the $ exemplar was removed from the schema. DO NOT silently reverse: (1) all 5 modes sonnet-4-6; (2) Types 1&2 (freeform + post-fix) append withLocaleContext(userLocale,userCurrency,userRegion) and carry NO hardcoded $ in parts_cost; (3) Types 1&2 create() is wrapped in a 3-attempt retry (they bypass callClaudeWithRetry for the photo-multipart path); (4) backend fix_ref allowlist (17 ids) must stay a subset of frontend FIXES keys. Frontend-only: result card is single-column/flex-wrap (mobile-safe); SHOP_COSTS savings use sym + fixed reference numbers (deliberate). See audit/BIKEMEDIC-NOTES.md.
_meta.rate_limit_note: Backend limits 4 req/min; check:golden runs the 3 cases sequentially and fits.

input.symptom: Chain skips under load on the third gear from the top, indexed shifting, happened gradually

output.diagnosis: Worn chain or cassette causing skipping under load on a specific sprocket. The gradual onset strongly points to drivetrain wear rather than a cable or indexing issue.
output.explanation: As a chain wears, its pitch elongates slightly. This elongated chain no longer meshes cleanly with the sprocket teeth, which have been machined to accept a new chain's geometry. Under load — when you're pushing power through the drivetrain — the chain rides up over the worn teeth and skips. The fact that it's isolated to one specific gear (third from the top) is a classic tell: that sprocket sees the most use on a 2x road setup and wears fastest.
likely_causes: Worn chain and/or cassette — the most common cause by far on indexed drivetrains; gradual onset under load on a heavily-used sprocket is the textbook presentation
Cable stretch or housing contamination causing the derailleur to sit slightly off-index, forcing the chain to run at an angle and skip under load
Bent derailleur hanger causing the derailleur to track improperly through the cassette, with the problem becoming more pronounced under load
fix_steps: SAFETY FIRST: Do not sprint or stand out of the saddle until resolved — a chain skip under hard load can pitch you over the bars. Avoid that gear entirely if you must ride before fixing it.

Check chain wear: use a chain wear indicator tool (CC-2 style or similar). On an 11-speed chain, replace at 0.5% wear; on 10-speed or below, at 0.75%. If worn, a new chain alone may not fix it if the cassette is also worn — see step 3.

Inspect the third sprocket from the top visually: worn teeth will look 'shark-finned' or hooked rather than symmetrical. Run your thumbnail against the teeth — it should catch evenly. If teeth are visibly hooked, the cassette needs replacing.

If chain wear is confirmed: replace the chain. Fit it to the bike and test in that specific sprocket. If skipping persists, the sprocket is worn and the cassette must also be replaced. A new chain on a worn cassette will skip immediately.

If chain wear is within tolerance: check rear derailleur indexing. Shift to the problematic sprocket, then use the barrel adjuster (usually on the derailleur or cable stop at the frame) and turn it anti-clockwise in half-turn increments to add cable tension. Test under light load after each adjustment.

If indexing adjustment doesn't resolve it, inspect the derailleur hanger: remove the rear wheel and sight along the derailleur from behind the bike — the derailleur body should be perfectly parallel to the sprockets. A bent hanger requires a hanger alignment tool to correct.
Final verification: ride and shift through all gears on both chainrings under moderate load. All shifts should be positive and silent. Pay particular attention to the previously problematic sprocket.
tools_needed: Chain wear indicator tool (e.g. Park Tool CC-2 or similar)
Chain breaker tool or quick-link pliers if replacing chain
Cassette lockring tool (matching your cassette — Shimano HG, SRAM XD, etc.)
Adjustable spanner or 24mm spanner for cassette lockring
Flat-head screwdriver or 5mm Allen key for barrel adjuster (varies by groupset)
output.time_estimate: 30–60 minutes for a full chain and cassette swap including cleaning; 5–10 minutes if it's an indexing adjustment only.
output.parts_cost: A replacement chain costs roughly £10–£25 depending on speed (e.g. Shimano HG601 11-speed is around £18–£22 at UK retailers as of mid-2026). A replacement cassette runs £20–£60 for mid-range (e.g. Shimano 105 or Tiagra). Budget £35–£80 if both are needed — which is likely if the chain is significantly worn.
output.pro_tip: Always measure chain wear before replacing a cassette — if you swap the cassette but keep a worn chain, you'll destroy the new cassette within a few hundred miles. Replace both together when the chain is at or past the wear limit.
output.shop_visit: Take it to a shop if the derailleur hanger appears bent and you don't have a hanger alignment tool, or if skipping persists after a new chain and cassette — that may indicate a worn chainring, derailleur pulley wear, or a bent derailleur cage.
output.prevention: Check chain wear every 500–800 km using a wear indicator — it takes under two minutes. Replacing a chain at the correct wear interval (before it's overdue) is the single best way to protect your cassette and extend drivetrain life significantly.
next_steps: Check chain wear with a wear indicator tool today — this will confirm or rule out the most likely cause before spending any money
If chain is worn, replace chain and test; replace cassette too if the sprocket teeth are visibly shark-finned or skipping continues with the new chain
If chain wear is within tolerance, adjust rear derailleur indexing via the barrel adjuster; if that fails, inspect and realign the derailleur hanger or visit a shop
related_issues: Chainring wear — check the chainring teeth on both rings for shark-finning, particularly the inner ring if you use a compact double
Derailleur pulley wear — jockey wheels with 5,000+ km on them can contribute to poor shifting; replace if teeth are visibly worn or the wheels have noticeable side play
Cable and housing condition — road grime and moisture degrade indexed shifting over time; consider a full cable and housing refresh if the bike has not had one in 18–24 months
Chain lube and cleanliness — a dry or dirty chain accelerates wear; clean and re-lube while the drivetrain is apart

input.symptom: clicking noise from the bottom when I pedal hard

output.reasoning: A clicking noise specifically under hard pedaling effort is the classic presentation of a loose or worn bottom bracket, loose pedal, or loose crank arm — all in the pedals/crank/BB category — because the click is load-dependent and originates at the primary power transfer point.
output.suggested_first_question: Does the click happen once per pedal revolution at the same point in the stroke, or is it more random — and does it stop completely when you stop pedaling and coast?

output.title: Spring Maintenance Checklist
output.summary: After recent wet and muddy rides at 3,200 miles, this gravel rig needs a thorough drivetrain and brake inspection before spring riding ramps up — grit infiltration, accelerated chain wear, and contaminated disc brakes are the top concerns right now.

tasks.task: Measure chain wear and replace if stretch exceeds 0.75% on your 1x drivetrain.
tasks.reason: At 3,200 miles with recent muddy riding, chain wear accelerates fast — a worn chain on a 1x system quickly destroys the single cassette, which is an expensive replacement.

tasks.task: Inspect disc brake pads on both front and rear calipers and replace if compound is below 1.5mm.
tasks.reason: Wet and muddy conditions dramatically accelerate pad wear and can embed grit into the pad compound, reducing braking power and scoring rotors.

tasks.task: Clean rotors and calipers with isopropyl alcohol and inspect for brake squeal or reduced modulation.
tasks.reason: Mud and road spray from recent rides likely contaminated braking surfaces — contaminated rotors are a safety issue and will ruin new pads quickly if not addressed first.

tasks.task: Refresh tubeless sealant in both tires — drain old sealant, inspect tire bead and casing, and add fresh sealant.
tasks.reason: Sealant dries out and loses sealing ability over a winter and early spring season; at this mileage the casing should also be inspected for cuts or sidewall wear from gravel and mud.

tasks.task: Degrease the full drivetrain — chain, chainring, cassette, and derailleur pulleys — then re-lube with a wet or all-conditions lubricant suited to spring riding.
tasks.reason: Muddy rides leave abrasive grit embedded in the drivetrain that acts as a grinding paste; running it longer without a full degrease accelerates wear on all contact surfaces.

tasks.task: Check headset for play and gritty rotation by squeezing the front brake, rocking the bike fore-aft, and rotating the bars through the full range.
tasks.reason: Gravel and wet riding force grit past headset seals — a gritty or loose headset causes handling instability and bearing damage that worsens quickly if ignored.

tasks.task: Inspect the bottom bracket for creaking or lateral play under pedaling load.
tasks.reason: Water and mud infiltration during recent wet rides is a leading cause of BB bearing deterioration on gravel bikes, and a creak at 3,200 miles is a strong indicator of worn or contaminated bearings.

tasks.task: Check both wheel hubs for lateral play by gripping each wheel and rocking it side-to-side while the bike is stationary.
tasks.reason: Grit and water intrusion from muddy rides can accelerate hub bearing wear; catching loose bearings early prevents cone or cup damage that turns a simple adjustment into a rebuild.

tasks.task: Spin both wheels and check for lateral or radial trueness, inspecting spoke tension while you're at it.
tasks.reason: At 3,200 miles on gravel terrain, spokes settle and fatigue — a wheel slightly out of true from a muddy ride impact can become a structural problem if left unaddressed heading into heavier spring mileage.

tasks.task: Shift through all gears and check for ghost shifting, hesitation, or delayed engagement under load.
tasks.reason: Grit contamination in the rear derailleur and cable housing (if cable-actuated) or corrosion in electronic contacts can cause indexing to drift after wet and muddy rides on a 1x system where you have no front derailleur to compensate.
